coredata.dc:title: Metal-organic framework with dual-loading of nickel/nitrogen-doped carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles for fluorescence detection of fenitrothion in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Delnavaz, Elnaz
Amjadi, Mohammad
Farajzadeh, Mir Ali
coredata.dc:description: The development of precise methods for the residue assay of organophosphate toxin, fenitrothion, is of great significance in minimizing its side effects on human life and the environment. Herein, a magnetic fluorimetric nanosensor for fenitrothion was constructed via embedding the nickel and nitrogen-doped carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The prepared nanosensor combines the advantages of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (magnetic separation and enriching the target analyte) and ZIF-8 (excellent adsorption capability) with high sensitivity of Ni,N-carbon dots. Magnetic ZIF-8 nanocrystals were used as ideal adsorbents to catch and accumulate fenitrothion, and Ni,N-carbon dots were exploited as optical materials to produce stable and strong fluorescence signals. We observed that the fluorescence signal of the ZIF-based nanocomposite (with excitation/emission maxima at 340/450 nm) was dynamically quenched by fenitrothion via the electron transfer process. Hence, a platform was designed for the assay of fenitrothion with a detection limit of 0.8 nM. The practicability of this method was verified by the quantification of fenitrothion in real food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Metal ion-doped carbon dots
Pesticides
Sensor
Food analysis
Food composition

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ZIF-based nanocomposite as a sensing platform for fenitrothion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectrum of Ni,N-carbon dots as well as fluorescence spectrum of ZIF-based nanocomposite and fluorescence spectra of Ni,N-carbon dots at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 440 nm.
TEM image of (a) Fe3O4 nanoparticles, (b) Ni,N-carbon dots, and (c) ZIF-based nanocomposite.
(a) XRD pattern of Ni,N-carbon dots, (b) XRD pattern of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and (c) XRD patterns of the original ZIF-8 and ZIF-based nanocomposite.
VSM plots of ZIF-based nanocomposite and Fe3O4 nanoparticles.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZIF-based nanocomposite and ZIF-8.
UV–vis spectra of fenitrothion and Ni,N-carbon dots as well as excitation and emission spectra of Ni,N-carbon dots.
Stern-Volmer plots before and after correction of inner filter effect.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of ZIF-based nanocomposite in the presence of various amounts of fenitrothion at optimum conditions and (b) calibration plot for fenitrothion in the range from 0.9 nM to 120 nM.
Principle and steps of fenitrothion sensing.
Comparison of the analytical characteristics of some methods for fenitrothion determination.
Interferences of several coexisting species in the determination of fenitrothion (50 nM).

row.entry: Al3+, Zn2+, PO4 3-, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of fenitrothion in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Metal-organic framework with dual-loading of nickel/nitrogen-doped carbon dots and magnetic nanoparticles for fluorescence detection of fenitrothion in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of precise methods for the residue assay of organophosphate toxin, fenitrothion, is of great significance in minimizing its side effects on human life and the environment. Herein, a magnetic fluorimetric nanosensor for fenitrothion was constructed via embedding the nickel and nitrogen-doped carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The prepared nanosensor combines the advantages of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (magnetic separation and enriching the target analyte) and ZIF-8 (excellent adsorption capability) with high sensitivity of Ni,N-carbon dots. Magnetic ZIF-8 nanocrystals were used as ideal adsorbents to catch and accumulate fenitrothion, and Ni,N-carbon dots were exploited as optical materials to produce stable and strong fluorescence signals. We observed that the fluorescence signal of the ZIF-based nanocomposite (with excitation/emission maxima at 340/450 nm) was dynamically quenched by fenitrothion via the electron transfer process. Hence, a platform was designed for the assay of fenitrothion with a detection limit of 0.8 nM. The practicability of this method was verified by the quantification of fenitrothion in real food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate pesticides are widely utilized all over the world due to their high efficacy in combating pests and low durability in the environment (Yan et al., 2018). Currently, fenitrothion is the most preferred low-cost organophosphate pesticide for insect destruction in cotton, cereals, wheat, rice, fruits, vegetables, and so on (Bazmandegan-Shamili et al., 2018; Özcan et al., 2019; Larki, 2017; Nebu et al., 2018). Besides, the effectiveness of fenitrothion as an agent to control some diseases, including malaria, has been verified by the World Health Organization (Eskandari and Naderi-Darehshori, 2012; Ulusoy et al., 2020). However, its long-term application pollutes soils and aquatic environments and has a detrimental impact on human health (Eskandari and Naderi-Darehshori, 2012; Kokulnathan et al., 2021; Özcan et al., 2019). The UK Environmental Agency has documented fenitrothion in the Red List of pesticides (Canevari et al., 2016). Therefore, developing analytical strategies for the precise assay of fenitrothion residues is a significant challenge for researchers. Numerous investigations have been reported for the analysis of fenitrothion, such as gas chromatography (Malhat et al., 2017), high-performance liquid chromatography (Ulusoy et al., 2020), electrochemistry (Cesana et al., 2021), chemiluminescence (Liu et al., 2007), colorimetric (Larki, 2017), and fluorescence (Nebu et al., 2018).

Carbon dots have inspired enormous research in various fields because of their unique attributes such as stable, intense, and tunable fluorescence emission, low toxicity, excellent aqueous solubility, and so on (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020; Shokri and Amjadi, 2021; Wang et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018). Despite such excellent characteristics, low fluorescence quantum efficiency and insufficient surface functional groups still are serious challenges (Lin et al., 2018). Doping carbon dots with heteroatom is an efficient strategy to adjust their intrinsic features and give them new functionalities (Tejwan et al., 2021). Especially, metal-doped carbon dots have become a research focus due to their exceptional optical features. Various carbon dots doped with metals such as Fe (Huang et al., 2019), Co (Delnavaz and Amjadi, 2021), Ni (Tu et al., 2020), and so on have been prepared for different applications.

Metal-organic frameworks are a category of porous materials prepared via the assembling of metallic ions with organic linkers in proper solvents (Abdelhamid, 2021; Abdelhamid et al., 2022; Abdelhamid and Mathew, 2022, Li and Huo, 2015). They have prominent characteristics, such as enormous surface area, high porosity, tunable pore sizes, excellent mechanical and thermal stability, and a large variety in the structure (Chen et al., 2014; Li et al., 2020). Metal-organic frameworks can serve as potential host matrices for various guest species; their structure hampers the aggregation of these species, hence enhancing their performance (Ma et al., 2018). Up to now, various species such as quantum dots and carbon dots have been incorporated in metal-organic frameworks to get luminescent composites with enhanced optical features for fluorimetric analysis (Asadi et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2022). Moreover, the loading of magnetic nanoparticles into metal-organic frameworks has sparked great interest due to their excellent adsorption capacity and facile separation of the nanoparticles from the solution by an external magnetic field (Luo et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2018).

Owing to the importance of developing a simple and rapid method for monitoring the ultra-trace levels of fenitrothion in food and environmental samples, we decided to construct a magnetic fluorescent nanosensor for this analyte. In this sensor Ni,N-carbon dots have been used for the first time as a highly sensitive fluorescent reporter. This property of doped carbon dots was combined with the accumulating ability of metal-organic framework and enriching and separating ability of Fe3O4 nanoparticles to produce a nanosensor for fenitrothion, which has superior sensitivity and lower detection limit than most of previously reported methods.
Fluorescence and absorption measurements were conducted using an FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan) and a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Sydney, Australia), respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by placing one or two drops of sample solutions onto a copper grid and drying overnight at room temperature. Samples were investigated using a Leo 906 instrument (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) operating at 100 kV.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by a D5000 X-ray diffractometer (Siemens, California, USA). For this purpose, the powder samples were placed in the sample holder, and the XRD patterns were recorded at room temperature using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) at 30 kV and 20 mA. Diffraction data were collected in the range of 2θ = 10–80° and 2θ = 5–30° at a scan rate of 2.4° min−1 with a step size of 0.02°.

The magnetic properties of the synthesized nanoparticles were investigated by a Kashan Kavir vibrating sample magnetometer (Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co., Kashan, Iran) in a 1.0 Tesla magnetic field. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was employed to examine the porosity and surface area of the synthesized nanocomposite by nitrogen adsorption/desorption studies using a Belsorp mini II analyzer (BEL, Tokyo, Japan). First, the sample was degassed to remove the moisture and gases that fill the sample’s surface. Afterward, the prepared sample was analyzed by BET.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was obtained by Mira 3 instrument (Tescan Co., Brno, Czech Republic) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX). For SEM analysis, the sample was loaded onto the carbon tape and then coated with a thin layer of gold particles using a sputter coating machine. SEM imaging was conducted under vacuum by X-rays with 15 kV energy.

Nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (97 %), citric acid monohydrate (99.5–100.5 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (>99 %), diethylenetriamine (98 %), 2-methylimidazole (>=99 %), ammonium hydroxide (28 %), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (>99 %), and iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (99–102 %) were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Fenitrothion (99.8 %) was obtained from GYAH Co. (Karaj, Iran). Fenitrothion stock solution (1000 mg/L) was prepared by adding a minimum volume of methanol and diluting it with deionized water. Deionized water (aqua MAX-Ultra 370, Young Lin Instrument Co., Korea) was applied throughout this research.

Different types of carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared in accordance with the previous methods with minor changes. All steps are available in the Electronic Supplementary Materials (ESM).

The ZIF-based nanocomposite was prepared via a method reported previously (Shokri and Amjadi, 2021). Initially, 20 mL of methanolic Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.9870 mmol) solution was rapidly poured into 20 mL of 2-methylimidazole (7.904 mmol) solution under magnetic stirring. Then, 800 µL of Ni,N-carbon dots, and 3.0 mL of Fe3O4 nanoparticle suspension were added to the mixture immediately and left to react for 1 h under stirring. The generated precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with methanol. Finally, the obtained composite was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water for further use.

The analysis of fenitrothion was performed as follows: 1.3 mL (4.3 mg) of the ZIF-based nanocomposite suspension and 350 µL of 1.0 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) were poured into a centrifuge tube. Then, fenitrothion solution with various concentrations (5 mL) was added, and the mixture was vortexed for 2 min. Next, the supernatant was discarded after the magnetic separation, and the precipitate was dispersed in 500 µL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0). Finally, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.
Parameters affecting the method’s sensitivity, including pH of the solution, amount of the nanocomposite, and response time, were studied and optimized. The details of procedures are given ESM.

Wastewater and juice samples were filtered using a filter paper (ALBET, DP 135 125, Barcelona, Spain) and diluted (3-fold for wastewater and 16-fold for juice samples) with deionized water. For recovery analyses, 5.0 mL of each sample was spiked with 50 µL of fenitrothion standard solution (5.0 or 15.0 µM) before any pretreatment and then evaluated based on the procedure mentioned above. The average recovery was acquired by measuring three replicates for each concentration.

The ZIF-based nanocomposite was prepared via a facile one-pot procedure by incorporating Ni,N-carbon dots as a fluorescent agent, and Fe3O4 nanoparticles as an enriching agent into metal-organic framework. UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy were accomplished to illustrate the optical characteristics of Ni,N-carbon dots ( Fig. 1). Two peaks at about 250 and 360 nm in the UV–vis spectrum are assigned to the π–π * and n–π * transitions, respectively. Fig. 1 indicates that the fluorescence peak position varies with the excitation wavelength. Also, as shown in Fig. 1, the fluorescence spectrum of Ni,N-carbon dots (λex = 340 nm) has a higher intensity than the fluorescence spectrum of nanocomposite under the same conditions. The lower intensity of nanocomposite is due to the encapsulation of carbon dots into ZIF-8. The quantum yield of Ni,N-carbon dots was computed to be 59.8 % using quinine sulfate as a standard. Also, Fig. S1 shows that both ZIF-based nanocomposite and Ni,N-carbon dots exhibit bright blue light under 365 nm UV illumination, respectively.

The morphology of ZIF-based nanocomposite was characterized by SEM and TEM images.
Figs. 2 and 3c show that the synthesized nanocomposite exhibits a rhombic dodecahedral structure with a mean size of approximately 100 nm. Also, TEM images (Fig. 3a and b) indicate that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Ni,N-CDs have spherical morphology. They are hardly seen in the TEM image of nanocomposite even if using local amplification.

The crystalline structures of the ZIF-based nanocomposite, original ZIF-8, Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and Ni,N-carbon dots were studied by XRD technique. As pictured in Fig. 4a, the XRD pattern of carbon dots reveals two peaks situated at 2θ = 22.2° and 37.3°, which demonstrates the highly disordered carbon atoms arranged randomly (Hara et al., 2004). However, no discernible peak of the Ni element is seen in the XRD pattern, which is likely caused by the low content of Ni in the doped carbon dots. The diffraction peaks of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 4b) are observed at 30.1° (220), 35.3° (311), 43.0° (400), 53.5° (422), 57.0° (511), and 62.6° (440), which prove the successful synthesis of crystalline magnetic nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2019). Fig. 4c portrays the crystalline structure of the original ZIF-8 and ZIF-based nanocomposite, which are consistent with the previously reported investigations (Zhang et al., 2018b, 2018a). Both nanocrystals exhibit a diffraction peak at 2θ = 7.2° (110), confirming the rhombic dodecahedron shape of the materials (Shokri and Amjadi, 2021; Xie et al., 2021). Moreover, similar diffraction peaks are observed in the XRD patterns of the original ZIF-8 and ZIF-based nanocomposite, corroborating that the distribution of guest molecules does not disorganize the crystalline structure of the ZIF-8. However, it is worth mentioning that the peaks of Ni,N-carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles are not seen in the XRD pattern of ZIF-based nanocomposite, which is presumably due to the low concentrations of these particles and the weak intensity of their diffraction peaks compared to strong peaks of ZIF-8 (Asadi et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2021).
The result of EDX analysis is depicted in Fig. S2, illustrating the existence of C, O, Zn, Fe, N, and Ni elements in the structure of synthesized ZIF-based nanocomposite.

The VSM technique was used for characterization of magnetic behavior of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and the synthesized nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 5, the saturation magnetization (Ms) of ZIF-based nanocomposite is 5.97 emu g−1, which is adequate for magnetic separation. The lower Ms value of ZIF-based nanocomposite compared to Fe3O4 nanoparticles (73.87 emu g−1) stems from the formation of the ZIF shell on the surface of Fe3O4. Moreover, showing a sigmoidal curve without a hysteresis loop affirms the superparamagnetic natures of both samples (Kolhatkar et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2012).

The permanent porosity of ZIF-based nanocomposite was investigated by the BET gas-sorption method. As illustrated in Fig. 6, the ZIF-based nanocomposite displays a typical Type-I BET isotherm, which confirms the microporous nature of the nanocomposite. Besides, the formation of a hysteresis loop near P/P0 = 1 in the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the nanocomposite can be attributed to the existence of interparticle macroporosity and mesoporosity between ZIF-8 crystals (Zhang et al., 2018a). The BET surface area of nanocomposite was calculated to be 1165 m2 g−1 which is lower than the value for original ZIF-8 (1486 m2 g−1), corroborating the incorporation of carbon dots and Fe3O4 nanoparticles into ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ZIF-based nanocomposite as a sensing platform for fenitrothion
ce:section.ce:para: We utilized the ZIF-based nanocomposite for the sensitive assay of fenitrothion based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence of the nanocomposite. Scheme 1 illustrates the principle and steps of the sensing procedure. In this nanosensor, carbon dots act as fluorescence reporters; their fluorescence intensity decreases with the analyte concentration. Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles act as enriching materials, which concentrate the analyte and allow detection of its lower concentrations. Moreover, their magnetic property allows the easy separation of nanocomposite from initial solution. Finally, the metal-organic framework is a host material that holds the components together, and also accumulates the analyte and enhances its contact with other components.

To achieve the highest sensitivity for fenitrothion determination, the effect of different parameters such as pH of the solution, amount of the composite, and response time was evaluated. The results are given in Fig. S3-S5 (ESM). Accordingly, the highest quenching response was acquired at pH 6.0 with phosphate buffer, 1.3 mL of the composite solution, and a response time of 2.0 min

We accomplished a series of investigations to gain an insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of fenitrothion. Firstly, it can be seen from Fig. 7 that there is no spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the Ni,N-carbon dots with the absorption spectrum of fenitrothion. Thus, the energy transfer is not a possible route for fluorescence suppression.

Fig. 7. reveals a small spectral overlap between the excitation spectrum of carbon dots and the absorption spectrum of the quencher. Therefore, we examined the inner filter effect (IFE) as a possible cause of fluorescence quenching. For this purpose, we corrected the fluorescence signals according to Eq. (1):
(1)
F
corr
F
obs
=
10
A
ex
+
A
em
2
where Fcorr and Fobs refer to the corrected and observed fluorescence intensity, respectively, and Aex and Aem are the absorbance of the solution at the excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively. The Stern-Volmer plots ( Fig. 8) using observed and corrected fluorescence intensities do not differ markedly, demonstrating that the fluorescence suppression does not stem from the IFE process.

As depicted in Fig. 7, the absorption peak of fenitrothion is close to the bandgap of the Ni,N-carbon dots; thus, electrons from the conduction band of the excited-state carbon dots can transfer to the LUMO of fenitrothion molecules as electron acceptors. The presence of a strong electron-withdrawing nitro group in the structure of fenitrothion further proves that electron transfer is the dominant pathway for fluorescence quenching.

To further study the quenching process, Stern–Volmer analyses were accomplished. The investigations were carried out at different temperatures, and the KSV values were computed using Eq. (2):
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is fenitrothion concentration, and F and F0 are fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of fenitrothion, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S6, KSV increases gradually with temperature, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching has a dynamic nature. Therefore, it can be deduced that fenitrothion quenches the fluorescence of nanocomposite through electron transfer by a dynamic process.

The introduced nanosensor was used for the determination of fenitrothion. Fig. 9a exhibits the fluorescence spectra of nanocomposite in the presence of various concentrations of fenitrothion. Fig. 8b shows the linear relation between F0/F values and fenitrothion concentration in the range of 0.9–120 nM. The regression equation can be expressed as F0/F = 0.0121 C + 1.0003, where F0 and F are fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of fenitrothion, respectively, and C is the fenitrothion concentration (nM). The limit of detection and quantification were estimated to be 0.8 nM and 2.9 nM, respectively. The precision of the fluorimetric assay was evaluated by replicate determining of 20 nM fenitrothion (n = 5), and the RSD was computed to be 2.8 %, confirming the favorable repeatability of the assay.

The quenching effect of fenitrothion on the signal of the non-magnetic ZIF-based nanosensor was also assessed. We observed that the linear range and limit of detection of the non-magnetic sensor were higher (10–1000 nM with a detection limit of 8.7 nM) than the magnetic sensor, implying the significant role of magnetic property in the performance of the nanosensor.

The comparison between the analytical characteristics of our nanosensor with some reported techniques for fenitrothion determination exhibits that our method has a reasonable linear range and a much better detection limit than most other techniques ( Table 1).

Selectivity of the proposed sensing platform was investigated toward some ions, which can interfere in the determination of fenitrothion ( Table 2). The tolerable limits in Table 2 (at a relative error of ≤5 %) show that there is no considerable interference from foreign species for fenitrothion analysis in the real samples. Moreover, the effect of different pesticides on the fluorescence signal of the nanosensor was assessed, and the results are graphically illustrated in Fig. S7. As can be seen, fenitrothion shows the highest quenching effect on the fluorescence signal, due to the presence of an electron-withdrawing nitro group in its structure.

The alidity of the proposed nanosensor was evaluated for the analysis of real samples. Different quantities of the standard fenitrothion solution were spiked to the samples and then analyzed by the mentioned procedure. As compiled in Table 3, the recoveries vary in the range of 94.6–102 %, and the values of Student’s t-statistic are below the critical value for all samples. These data prove the applicability of the proposed nanosensor for the analysis of fenitrothion in real samples.

In summary, fluorescent Ni,N-carbon dots, and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were incorporated simultaneously into ZIF-8 via a simple one-pot synthesis procedure. The prepared nanosensor has the advantages of magnetic separation and high adsorption capability. The obtained ZIF-based nanocomposite was used as a selective and sensitive sensor for fluorimetric assay of fenitrothion with a low detection limit of 0.8 nM. Therefore, this nanocomposite can be considered a promising nanosensor with excellent performance for fenitrothion determination in real samples.

Elnaz Delnavaz: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Mir Ali Farajzadeh: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2022.104873.
